---
title: "The `summarize()` Function"
subtitle: "CIS 351:"
date: ""
author: ""
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    number-sections: true
---

## Load Packages and Data

```{r}
#| message: false
library()

library()

library()

library()

dogs <- read_csv() |>
  clean_names()

earthquakes <- read_csv() |>
  clean_names()

fastfood <- read_csv() |>
  clean_names()

water_quality <- read_csv() |> 
  clean_names()
```

## Calculating Summary Statistics

Previously, we saw that the `summary()` function returns the five-number summary (along with the mean and the number of missing values) for a numeric variable. In contrast, the `summarize()` function allows us to create customized summaries using statistics of our own choosing. More importantly, `summarize()` can easily perform calculations within groups, while doing the same with `summary()` generally requires first splitting the data into separate groups.

:::{.callout-note icon='false'}
## Example 1.

Calculate the sample size, mean, and standard deviation of the penguins' body mass.

_Solution._
```{r}
  
```
:::

:::{.callout-note icon='false'}
## Example 2.

Repeat the calculations from Example 1, but group the results by penguin species.

_Solution._

```{r}
  
```
:::

:::{.callout-note icon='false'}
## Example 3.

Repeat the calculations from Example 2, grouping first by species and then by sex. Sort the results by sepcies and sex.

_Solution._

```{r}
  
```
:::

## Appendix

Belwo is a list of new functions introduced in this section.

| Function      | Description                                         |
|---------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| `summarize()` | Computes summary statistics for a data frame, optionally by group. |
| `arrange()`   | Sorts rows based on one or more variables.          |
: {.striped}
